1: A revelation of God: "The heavens declare the glory of God, and the expanse proclaims his handiwork." The creation's witness to the Creator is the foundational claim of general revelation. Paul in Romans 1:20 builds on this: "since the creation of the world God's invisible qualities... have been clearly seen, being understood from what has been made, so that people are without excuse." The Christ who is "the exact representation of his being" (Hebrews 1:3) is the ultimate expression of what the heavens declare — the glory of God made visible in a person.
2: A revelation of God: "Day after day pours out its speech, and night after night reveals knowledge." The unceasing testimony of creation — day and night without pause — is the continuous revelation that precedes and accompanies the special revelation of Scripture (Psalms 19:7–11) and the ultimate revelation in Christ. John 1:9 ("The true light that gives light to everyone was coming into the world") is the fulfillment of creation's light-witness: the light of each day is a shadow of the Light who is Christ.
3: A revelation of God: "There is no speech, there are no words — their voice cannot be heard." The paradox of creation's testimony: it speaks without words, conveys without language. The Word of God (John 1:1-3, 14) who created all things is the one whose creative speech underlies the voiceless testimony of the heavens. The speechless proclamation of creation and the spoken proclamation of the Word-made-flesh are two modes of the same divine self-revelation, reaching their personal fullness in Christ.
4: A direct citation: "Yet their proclamation goes out through all the earth, and their message to the ends of the world." Paul quotes this verse in Romans 10:18 — "Did they not hear? Of course they did: 'Their voice has gone out into all the earth, their words to the ends of the world.'" Paul applies the creation-witness of Psalms 19:4 to the apostolic proclamation of the gospel: the universal speech of creation becomes the model for the universal speech of the gospel. Christ as the fulfillment of both creations (first and new) sends preachers who proclaim as broadly as the heavens declare.
5: A shadow: "Which rises like a bridegroom leaving his chamber, and rejoices like a champion eager to run his course." The sun as a bridegroom and champion is ANE royal imagery applied to creation's most powerful visible element. The NT applies the bridegroom image to Christ specifically: Matthew 9:15 ("the guests of the bridegroom cannot mourn while he is with them"); John 3:29 ("The bride belongs to the bridegroom"); Revelation 19:7 ("the wedding of the Lamb has come"). The bridegroom-sun is a shadow of the Bridegroom who is the light of the world (John 8:12).
6: A shadow: "Its rising is from one end of the heavens, and its circuit reaches the other end; nothing is hidden from its heat." The all-reaching heat of the sun — nothing hidden from it — is the type of the omniscient presence of Christ. Hebrews 4:13 ("Nothing in all creation is hidden from God's sight. Everything is uncovered and laid bare before the eyes of him to whom we must give account") and Revelation 1:14 ("his eyes were like blazing fire") show Christ as the one whose searching gaze, like the sun's heat, reaches everything.
7: A type: "The instruction of the LORD is perfect, reviving the soul; the testimony of the LORD is trustworthy, making the simple wise." The Torah's life-giving, soul-reviving perfection is fulfilled in Christ who embodies Torah. John 5:39-40: "You study the Scriptures diligently because you think that in them you have eternal life — these are the very Scriptures that testify about me, yet you refuse to come to me to have life." The life that Torah points to is the life that Christ gives. He does not abolish the Torah's function; he is its fulfillment (Matthew 5:17).
8: A type: "The precepts of the LORD are right, bringing joy to the heart; the commandment of the LORD is radiant, enlightening the eyes." The Torah's joy-producing and eye-opening qualities are what the New Covenant promise in Jeremiah 31:33 reframes: "I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts." Christ, through the Spirit, writes the Torah that Psalm 19 praises on the inner person — producing genuine joy and illumination from within, not merely the external commandment.
9: A type: "The fear of the LORD is pure, enduring forever; the judgments of the LORD are true and altogether righteous." The "fear of the LORD" that endures forever — the covenant disposition that produces wisdom — finds its fullest expression in Christ, who "grew in wisdom" (Luke 2:52) and "feared God" (Hebrews 5:7: he was heard because of his reverent submission). The eternal endurance of the divine judgments that are "altogether righteous" is grounded in Christ, through whom the final judgment will be righteous (Acts 17:31).
10: A theme: "They are more desirable than gold... and sweeter than honey, than honey dripping from the comb." The surpassing value of divine instruction — more than gold, sweeter than honey — is the wisdom tradition's highest commendation. Christ as the wisdom of God (1 Corinthians 1:24, 30) is what the Torah's wisdom pointed to. The "honey from the comb" sweetness of Psalm 19 is the type of the "good news" (euangelion) — literally, good-tasting message — that Christ brings and embodies.
11: A theme: "Moreover, your servant is warned by them; in keeping them there is great reward." The Torah's function of warning and rewarding the covenant servant anticipates the role of Scripture in the Christian life. 2 Timothy 3:16-17 ("All Scripture is God-breathed and is useful for teaching, rebuking, correcting and training in righteousness") restates Psalm 19's logic. The "great reward (ʿēqeb rāb)" of keeping the divine instruction is ultimately the reward of eternal life in Christ (Matthew 5:12; 1 Corinthians 3:14).
12: A revelation of God: "Who can understand his own errors? Cleanse me from sins I cannot see." The acknowledgment of hidden sins — failures the speaker cannot even perceive — establishes the radical need for divine cleansing beyond self-knowledge. 1 John 1:7-9 ("the blood of Jesus, his Son, purifies us from all sin... if we confess our sins, he is faithful and just and will forgive us our sins and purify us from all unrighteousness") is the answer to Psalms 19:12. The cleansing from hidden sins is one of the functions of Christ's atoning work.
13: A type: "Keep your servant also from willful sins; let them not have mastery over me. Then I will be blameless and clean of great rebellion." The prayer against willful sins and their mastery over the person anticipates the NT's theology of sin's dominion and the freedom Christ brings. Romans 6:12-14 ("Do not let sin reign in your mortal body... sin shall no longer be your master, because you are not under the law, but under grace") is the answer to the prayer of Psalms 19:13: the freedom from sin's mastery is the work of Christ and the Spirit.
14: A direct connection: "May the words of my mouth and the meditation of my heart be pleasing in your sight, O LORD, my rock and my redeemer." The final title — "my redeemer (gōʾēlî)" — applies the kinsman-redeemer concept to YHWH. The gōʾēl is the nearest male relative who has the right and duty to redeem family members from slavery or poverty (Leviticus 25:25-28; Ruth 3-4). The NT applies the redeemer concept to Christ throughout: Galatians 3:13 ("Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law"), Titus 2:14 ("who gave himself for us to redeem us from all wickedness"). YHWH as gōʾēl is fulfilled in Christ who becomes kin to redeem humanity.
